title: One place past the last
checkLabels.t9: Past the end still gives undefined
checkLabels.t12: The last index comes from the length
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The first two lines in the console are NaN and undefined. The numbers are there, the total is not.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
why.still-past-end: The loop still reaches the length.
hint2.still-past-end: Five values, indexes 0 to 4. The length is 5 — an index that does not exist.
hint3.still-past-end: The condition has to stop before the length, not at it.
why.negative-index: There is still a negative index.
hint2.negative-index: Minus one does not mean the last one. In some languages it does. Not here.
hint3.negative-index: The last index is one less than the length. It is worked out from it.
hint3.total-nan: If you get NaN, undefined got into the sum somewhere. A number plus undefined does not crash — it gives NaN.
hint4.total-nan: Check how far the counter goes. Its last round reads a place that is not there.
hint2.wrong-last: The five numbers are 4, 2, 6, 1 and 3. The last is a three.
hint3.wrong-last: Five values, so the last index is 4. That is the length minus one.
why.past-end-changed: The third line no longer shows undefined.
hint2.past-end-changed: It is there on purpose. It shows that reading past the end does not crash.
hint2.array-changed: The numbers and their order stay: 4, 2, 6, 1, 3. The loop gets fixed, not the data.
why.hardcoded-last: The last index is written by hand.
hint2.hardcoded-last: That four becomes wrong the day you add a sixth day.
hint3.hardcoded-last: Ask for the length and subtract one from it.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: The same five days. The loop walks the array and adds up. The console shows NaN.
blocks.1.text: The numbers are there. The loop runs. Nothing crashes, nothing warns — and the total is nothing.
blocks.2.text: The length is not an index
blocks.3.text: Five values means indexes 0 to 4. The length is 5, but there is no place numbered 5.
blocks.4.text: So the loop stops before the length, not at it. Less-than and less-than-or-equal differ by one character and by one round.
blocks.5.quote: The last index is always the length minus one. This is not learned once and for all — it is checked every time.
blocks.6.text: Past the end does not crash
blocks.7.text: Ask for a place that is not there and you do not get an error. You get undefined. The code carries calmly on.
blocks.9.text: The first line gives undefined. The second gives undefined cup — undefined turns into text and sticks. The third gives 3, and there is no size numbered 3. Three different wrong things and not one message.
blocks.11.text: undefined in an addition gives NaN. NaN plus anything gives NaN again. One extra round at the start poisons the whole total.
blocks.12.text: So NaN on the screen rarely means a mistake where you see it. It is a trace of something that happened earlier and did not crash.
blocks.14.text: Fix the loop so it stops in time. The total has to be 16.
blocks.15.text: The last value is taken from the length, not with a negative index and not with a hand-written four. The third line stays as it is — it is there on purpose.
